subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Welcome back to Ehel Academy Science. You are now in Year 8, and you already know a great deal about how living things are built and how they stay alive. This year we turn to one of the deepest questions in all of biology: how does life continue from one generation to the next? Every living thing eventually grows old and dies, yet life on Earth never runs out. The secret is reproduction — the way living things make new individuals like themselves. In this unit you will discover how plants, the quiet green organisms all around you, carry out this remarkable task.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 8 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Explain the difference between asexual and sexual reproduction in plants, and give examples of each.
Describe several methods of asexual reproduction in plants, including runners, bulbs, tubers, and how farmers use cuttings and grafting.
Name and label the main parts of an insect-pollinated flower and state the job of each part.
Explain the difference between the male parts (stamen) and the female parts (carpel) of a flower.
Describe pollination and distinguish between insect pollination and wind pollination.
Explain fertilisation in a flowering plant and what happens after it.
Describe how seeds and fruits form from a fertilised flower.
Explain why seed dispersal matters and describe the main methods plants use to spread their seeds.
Describe the conditions needed for germination and outline the life cycle of a flowering plant.
Explain how farmers use their knowledge of plant reproduction to grow better crops.

concepts.title: Think about the mango tree in a garden in Kismaayo, heavy…
concepts.explanation: Think about the mango tree in a garden in Kismaayo, heavy with fruit in the wet season. Think about the fields of maize and sorghum growing along the Jubba and Shabelle rivers, the tall acacia standing alone on the savanna, and the date palms of the dry north. Every one of these plants came from another plant before it. The mango you eat today holds inside it the seed of a mango tree that has not yet been born. When you spit out that seed and it lands in moist soil, you are holding the future of a whole tree in…
concepts.example: Understanding plant reproduction is not just interesting — it feeds the world. Every farmer along the rivers of East Africa depends on it. When a farmer near Jamaame plants maize, saves the best seeds for next season,…

concepts.title: Understanding plant reproduction is not just interesting —…
concepts.explanation: Understanding plant reproduction is not just interesting — it feeds the world. Every farmer along the rivers of East Africa depends on it. When a farmer near Jamaame plants maize, saves the best seeds for next season, grafts a sweet mango branch onto a strong rootstock, or takes a cutting from a healthy banana plant, he is using the science of plant reproduction to grow food for his family and community. The bananas, dates, mangoes, papayas, sorghum, and beans that fill the markets of Mogadishu and Hargeisa all…
concepts.example: As Muslims we are invited to look closely at the world and reflect on the care with which it was made. Few things reward this reflection more than a flower. A single flower is a tiny machine of astonishing design, built…

concepts.title: As Muslims we are invited to look closely at the world and…
concepts.explanation: As Muslims we are invited to look closely at the world and reflect on the care with which it was made. Few things reward this reflection more than a flower. A single flower is a tiny machine of astonishing design, built to bring together the beginnings of new life, often with the help of a bee, a bird, or the wind. The Qur'an draws our attention many times to the way dry earth comes alive with green growth after the rain. As you study this unit, let your curiosity be a form of reflecting on creation and a reason…

explorations.title: Dissecting a Flower
explorations.context: To take apart a real flower and find its main parts: the sepals, petals, stamens, and carpel.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down what you expect: How many petals do you think the flower has? Will you be able to find the male and female parts? Write your prediction, then test it.
explorations.answer: Which parts did you find inside the ring of petals — the male parts, the female parts, or both? From the petals and pollen you saw, do you think this flower is pollinated by insects or by wind? Give your reason. Why do you think the stigma is sticky?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To take apart a real flower and find its main parts: the sepals, petals, stamens, and carpel.

explorations.title: What Does a Seed Need to Germinate? (A Fair Test)
explorations.context: To find out which conditions a seed needs in order to germinate, by testing water, warmth, and air one at a time.
explorations.prompt: Write down which conditions you think a seed needs to sprout. Which of your jars do you predict will grow, and which will not? Record your predictions before you begin.
explorations.answer: In which jar or jars did the beans germinate? Jar B had no water. What does comparing Jar B with Jar A tell you about whether seeds need water? Jar C was full of water but the beans still did not grow. What condition was missing, and why does that matter for germination? Jar D was cold. What does…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out which conditions a seed needs in order to germinate, by testing water, warmth, and air one at a time.

explorations.title: Growing a New Plant Without Seeds (Asexual Reproduction)
explorations.context: To grow a whole new plant from just a part of a parent plant, showing asexual reproduction with your own eyes.
explorations.prompt: Do you think a piece of stem or a vegetable can grow into a whole new plant? Write your prediction. Will the new plant be the same as or different from the parent?
explorations.answer: How many parents did your new plant have? Is this asexual or sexual reproduction? Do you expect your new plant to be identical to the parent, or different? Explain why. Why is this method so useful to farmers who have found one especially good plant?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To grow a whole new plant from just a part of a parent plant, showing asexual reproduction with your own eyes.

explorations.title: Modelling Seed Dispersal
explorations.context: To model how seeds are dispersed by wind, and to see why light, winged seeds travel farther than heavy ones.
explorations.prompt: Which do you think will travel farther when dropped in moving air: a light seed with a 'wing', or a heavy seed with none? Write your prediction and your reason.
explorations.answer: Which object travelled farthest in the moving air? Which travelled the least? How does having a 'wing' or being light help a real seed spread away from its parent? The baobab and many grasses have light, wind-carried seeds. Using your results, explain why this helps them. Why did you repeat each…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To model how seeds are dispersed by wind, and to see why light, winged seeds travel farther than heavy ones.

methods.title: Dissecting a Flower
methods.example: To take apart a real flower and find its main parts: the sepals, petals, stamens, and carpel.
steps: Place the flower on the white paper so you can see the parts clearly against the pale background.
Gently pull off the green sepals at the base first. Count them and lay them to one side.
Next, carefully remove the petals one by one. Count them. Notice their colour and whether they have a scent.
Now look at the parts left in the centre. Find the stamens — the stalks with small bags (anthers) at the top. Touch an anther gently with your fingertip; the fine dust that comes off is pollen.
Find the carpel in the very centre. Look for the sticky stigma at the top, the style below it, and the swollen ovary at the base.

methods.title: What Does a Seed Need to Germinate? (A Fair Test)
methods.example: To find out which conditions a seed needs in order to germinate, by testing water, warmth, and air one at a time.
steps: You will set up four jars. Each jar changes only ONE condition, so you can see which condition matters. This is called a fair test: you keep everything the same except the one thing you are testing.
Put a layer of cotton wool or folded cloth in the bottom of each jar, and place five beans on top in each.
Jar A (the control): add a little water to dampen the cotton, and leave it on a warm shelf with air. This jar has water, warmth, and air.
Jar B (no water): add NO water. Leave the beans on dry cotton on the same warm shelf, with air.
Jar C (no air): fill this jar right to the top with water so the beans are completely covered and cannot get air. Keep it on the warm shelf.

methods.title: Growing a New Plant Without Seeds (Asexual Reproduction)
methods.example: To grow a whole new plant from just a part of a parent plant, showing asexual reproduction with your own eyes.
steps: Choose your plant part. If using a stem cutting, ask an adult to cut a piece about a hand-span long, just below a point where a leaf grows.
For a stem cutting: stand it in a jar with the cut end in a little water, or push it into moist soil in a pot.
For a sprouting potato: plant it in moist soil with the sprouting 'eye' pointing up.
For an onion or garlic: rest it on the top of a jar so the base just touches water, or plant it shallowly in soil.
Place your plant part on a sunny windowsill. Keep the water topped up or the soil damp.

methods.title: Modelling Seed Dispersal
methods.example: To model how seeds are dispersed by wind, and to see why light, winged seeds travel farther than heavy ones.
steps: Cut two or three small paper 'wings' — for example, thin strips or a simple two-bladed paper spinner — to model wind-dispersed seeds.
Stand in one spot. From the same height each time, drop each object in front of a fan or a breeze: a bean, a stone, and a paper 'winged seed'.
Measure how far from your feet each object lands.
Repeat each drop three times and record the distances so your test is fair.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 1
workedExamples.prompt: Asexual reproduction in plants produces offspring that are: (a) always different from the parent (b) identical to the parent (c) a mix of two parents (d) always larger than the parent
workedExamples.solution: (b) identical to the parent. Asexual reproduction uses one parent and no joining of sex cells, so offspring are clones.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: The male part of a flower is the: (a) carpel (b) stigma (c) stamen (d) sepal
workedExamples.solution: (c) stamen. The stamen (anther and filament) is the male part; remember 'stamen = man'.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: The part of the carpel that catches pollen is the: (a) ovary (b) style (c) filament (d) stigma
workedExamples.solution: (d) stigma. The sticky stigma at the top of the carpel catches pollen grains.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: Pollination is best described as: (a) the joining of sex cells (b) the transfer of pollen from anther to stigma (c) the growth of a seed (d) the ripening of a fruit
workedExamples.solution: (b) the transfer of pollen from anther to stigma. This is the definition of pollination; fertilisation comes later.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a feature of a wind-pollinated flower? (a) large bright petals (b) sweet nectar (c) feathery stigmas hanging outside (d) sticky pollen held inside
workedExamples.solution: (c) feathery stigmas hanging outside. Wind-pollinated flowers have feathery stigmas outside the flower to catch drifting pollen; the other options describe insect-pollinated flowers.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Fertilisation happens when: (a) a bee lands on a flower (b) the male sex cell joins the egg cell in the ovule (c) the petals fall off (d) the seed is dispersed
workedExamples.solution: (b) the male sex cell joins the egg cell in the ovule. That joining is fertilisation.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: After fertilisation, the ovule becomes the: (a) fruit (b) petal (c) seed (d) stamen
workedExamples.solution: (c) seed. Each fertilised ovule grows into a seed.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: After fertilisation, the ovary becomes the: (a) seed (b) fruit (c) root (d) stigma
workedExamples.solution: (b) fruit. The ovary ripens into the fruit that surrounds and protects the seeds.

practice.prompt: Asexual reproduction in plants produces offspring that are: (a) always different from the parent (b) identical to the parent (c) a mix of two parents (d) always larger than the parent
practice.answer: (b) identical to the parent. Asexual reproduction uses one parent and no joining of sex cells, so offspring are clones.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The male part of a flower is the: (a) carpel (b) stigma (c) stamen (d) sepal
practice.answer: (c) stamen. The stamen (anther and filament) is the male part; remember 'stamen = man'.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The part of the carpel that catches pollen is the: (a) ovary (b) style (c) filament (d) stigma
practice.answer: (d) stigma. The sticky stigma at the top of the carpel catches pollen grains.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Pollination is best described as: (a) the joining of sex cells (b) the transfer of pollen from anther to stigma (c) the growth of a seed (d) the ripening of a fruit
practice.answer: (b) the transfer of pollen from anther to stigma. This is the definition of pollination; fertilisation comes later.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a feature of a wind-pollinated flower? (a) large bright petals (b) sweet nectar (c) feathery stigmas hanging outside (d) sticky pollen held inside
practice.answer: (c) feathery stigmas hanging outside. Wind-pollinated flowers have feathery stigmas outside the flower to catch drifting pollen; the other options describe insect-pollinated flowers.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Fertilisation happens when: (a) a bee lands on a flower (b) the male sex cell joins the egg cell in the ovule (c) the petals fall off (d) the seed is dispersed
practice.answer: (b) the male sex cell joins the egg cell in the ovule. That joining is fertilisation.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: After fertilisation, the ovule becomes the: (a) fruit (b) petal (c) seed (d) stamen
practice.answer: (c) seed. Each fertilised ovule grows into a seed.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: After fertilisation, the ovary becomes the: (a) seed (b) fruit (c) root (d) stigma
practice.answer: (b) fruit. The ovary ripens into the fruit that surrounds and protects the seeds.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which three conditions does a seed need to germinate? (a) light, soil, and water (b) water, warmth, and oxygen (c) sunlight, warmth, and soil (d) oxygen, light, and food
practice.answer: (b) water, warmth, and oxygen. Seeds do not need light or soil to germinate; they feed on their own food store.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A farmer joins a sweet-fruiting shoot onto a strong-rooted plant. This is called: (a) pollination (b) germination (c) grafting (d) dispersal
practice.answer: (c) grafting. Joining a scion onto a rootstock is grafting, a form of asexual reproduction.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Maize and sorghum along the Jubba River are mainly pollinated by: (a) bees (b) wind (c) water (d) birds
practice.answer: (b) wind. Maize and sorghum are wind-pollinated cereal crops.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which is an example of asexual reproduction? (a) a bean seed sprouting (b) a mango grown from a stone (c) a banana plant sending up a new shoot (d) pollination of a sunflower
practice.answer: (c) a banana plant sending up a new shoot. That is one parent making a clone — asexual reproduction.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Dissecting a Flower
activities.materials: One large flower, such as a hibiscus, a bean flower, a pumpkin flower, or any big garden flower you can find; A sheet of white paper or a plate; A pair of tweezers or your fingers; A magnifying glass if you have one (not essential); A pencil for drawing
steps: Place the flower on the white paper so you can see the parts clearly against the pale background.
Gently pull off the green sepals at the base first. Count them and lay them to one side.
Next, carefully remove the petals one by one. Count them. Notice their colour and whether they have a scent.
Now look at the parts left in the centre. Find the stamens — the stalks with small bags (anthers) at the top. Touch an anther gently with your fingertip; the fine dust that comes off is pollen.
Find the carpel in the very centre. Look for the sticky stigma at the top, the style below it, and the swollen ovary at the base.

activities.title: What Does a Seed Need to Germinate? (A Fair Test)
activities.materials: About 20 dried beans (or maize grains, or lentils) from the kitchen; Four clean jars or cups; Cotton wool, or a folded cloth or paper towel; Water; A refrigerator (for the cold jar)
steps: You will set up four jars. Each jar changes only ONE condition, so you can see which condition matters. This is called a fair test: you keep everything the same except the one thing you are testing.
Put a layer of cotton wool or folded cloth in the bottom of each jar, and place five beans on top in each.
Jar A (the control): add a little water to dampen the cotton, and leave it on a warm shelf with air. This jar has water, warmth, and air.
Jar B (no water): add NO water. Leave the beans on dry cotton on the same warm shelf, with air.
Jar C (no air): fill this jar right to the top with water so the beans are completely covered and cannot get air. Keep it on the warm shelf.

activities.title: Growing a New Plant Without Seeds (Asexual Reproduction)
activities.materials: One of these: a fresh cassava stem cutting, a sprouting potato, an onion or garlic clove, or a stem cutting from a garden plant such as basil or mint; A cup, jar, or small pot; Water, or moist soil in a pot; A sunny windowsill
steps: Choose your plant part. If using a stem cutting, ask an adult to cut a piece about a hand-span long, just below a point where a leaf grows.
For a stem cutting: stand it in a jar with the cut end in a little water, or push it into moist soil in a pot.
For a sprouting potato: plant it in moist soil with the sprouting 'eye' pointing up.
For an onion or garlic: rest it on the top of a jar so the base just touches water, or plant it shallowly in soil.
Place your plant part on a sunny windowsill. Keep the water topped up or the soil damp.

activities.title: Modelling Seed Dispersal
activities.materials: A few small dried seeds or grains (beans, maize) to act as 'heavy seeds'; Paper and scissors to make 'winged seeds' (small paper spinners or paper strips); A small stone or bead as a very heavy 'seed'; A fan, or an open window on a breezy day, or simply your own breath; A tape measure or a length…
steps: Cut two or three small paper 'wings' — for example, thin strips or a simple two-bladed paper spinner — to model wind-dispersed seeds.
Stand in one spot. From the same height each time, drop each object in front of a fan or a breeze: a bean, a stone, and a paper 'winged seed'.
Measure how far from your feet each object lands.
Repeat each drop three times and record the distances so your test is fair.

terms: Sweet fleshy fruit, or hooks that catch on fur
Pods that dry and burst open
The female part: stigma + style + ovary (holds ovules).
Transfer of pollen from anther to stigma.
Joining of male and female sex cells inside the ovule.
Structure in the ovary holding the egg cell; becomes a seed after fertilisation.
A fertilised ovule: embryo + food store + seed coat.
A ripened ovary that surrounds and protects the seeds.
Scattering of seeds away from the parent plant.
commonMistakes: Saying pollination and fertilisation are the same.
Thinking only sweet things are fruits.
A fruit is defined by science, not taste.
Expecting a seed-grown tree to match its parent.
Seeds come from two parents and vary.
Thinking seeds need light and soil to sprout.
Seeds feed on their own food store first.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf grows mango trees near Afgooye. He wants every tree in his new orchard to give the same sweet fruit as his best tree. Should he grow them from seeds or by grafting? Explain your choice.
realProblems.answer: He should use grafting. Grafting is asexual reproduction, so every tree is a clone of the best tree and gives the same sweet fruit. Seeds would give varied fruit because they come from sexual reproduction.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: He should use grafting. Grafting is asexual reproduction, so every tree is a clone of the best tree and gives the same sweet fruit. Seeds would give varied fruit because they come from sexual reproduction.

realProblems.prompt: Halima grows maize in a wide block near the Shabelle River rather than in one thin row. Using what you know about how maize is pollinated, explain why a wide block gives a better harvest.
realProblems.answer: Maize is wind-pollinated. In a wide block, the wind can carry pollen from many plants to many others, so more flowers are pollinated and more grains form on each cob. A single thin row would let much pollen blow away without reaching other plants.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Maize is wind-pollinated. In a wide block, the wind can carry pollen from many plants to many others, so more flowers are pollinated and more grains form on each cob. A single thin row would let much pollen blow away without reaching other plants.

realProblems.prompt: A farmer plants bean seeds in soil that he keeps flooded with water all the time. The seeds rot and do not grow. Explain, using the conditions for germination, what went wrong.
realProblems.answer: The flooded soil left no oxygen (air) around the seeds. Seeds need oxygen to respire and release energy from their food store, so without air they could not germinate and rotted instead.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The flooded soil left no oxygen (air) around the seeds. Seeds need oxygen to respire and release energy from their food store, so without air they could not germinate and rotted instead.

realProblems.prompt: During the dry season, seeds lie in the baked soil and nothing grows. After the first heavy rains, the land turns green as many seeds germinate at once. Explain why the rain triggers germination.
realProblems.answer: Seeds need water to germinate. During the dry season there is no water, so the seeds stay dormant. The heavy rain provides the water (and the soil is warm), switching on the reactions inside the seeds, so many germinate together.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Seeds need water to germinate. During the dry season there is no water, so the seeds stay dormant. The heavy rain provides the water (and the soil is warm), switching on the reactions inside the seeds, so many germinate together.

realProblems.prompt: A goat eats the pods of an acacia tree and later drops the seeds far away in its droppings. Explain two ways this helps the acacia.
realProblems.answer: Two ways: the seed is carried far from the parent, so the seedling does not compete with the parent tree for water and space; and the seed passes through the goat unharmed and is dropped in droppings that act as natural fertiliser, giving it a good start.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Two ways: the seed is carried far from the parent, so the seedling does not compete with the parent tree for water and space; and the seed passes through the goat unharmed and is dropped in droppings that act as natural fertiliser, giving it a good start.

realProblems.prompt: A new plant disease arrives that can kill one grafted mango tree. Explain why it could destroy a whole orchard of grafted trees, but might not destroy a group of trees grown from seed. Use the idea of variation.
realProblems.answer: Grafted trees are all identical clones with the same instructions, so if the disease can kill one, it can kill them all — there is no variation to resist it. Seed-grown trees vary because of sexual reproduction, so some may happen to resist the disease and survive.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Grafted trees are all identical clones with the same instructions, so if the disease can kill one, it can kill them all — there is no variation to resist it. Seed-grown trees vary because of sexual reproduction, so some may happen to resist the disease and survive.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: State two differences between asexual and sexual reproduction.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Asexual reproduction has one parent and produces identical clones; sexual reproduction has two parents and produces varied offspring. (Any two clear differences.)

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the two parts of a stamen and say what each does.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The anther makes and holds the pollen; the filament is the stalk that holds the anther up.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the three parts of a carpel.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The stigma, the style, and the ovary.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between pollination and fertilisation.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Pollination is the transfer of pollen from anther to stigma. Fertilisation is the joining of the male and female sex cells inside the ovule, which happens after pollination.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Give two features of an insect-pollinated flower and explain how each helps.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Any two: bright petals (attract insects), scent (attracts insects), nectar (rewards and draws insects), sticky pollen (sticks to the insect's body). Each helps the insect carry pollen between flowers.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: List the three things found inside a seed.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: An embryo (tiny young plant), a food store, and a protective seed coat.

questions.question: Asexual reproduction in plants produces offspring that are:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: identical to the parent. Asexual reproduction uses one parent and no joining of sex cells, so offspring are clones.

questions.question: The male part of a flower is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: stamen. The stamen (anther and filament) is the male part; remember 'stamen = man'.

questions.question: The part of the carpel that catches pollen is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: stigma. The sticky stigma at the top of the carpel catches pollen grains.

questions.question: Pollination is best described as:
options: the transfer of pollen from anther to stigma
questions.answer: the transfer of pollen from anther to stigma
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the transfer of pollen from anther to stigma. This is the definition of pollination; fertilisation comes later.

questions.question: Which of these is a feature of a wind-pollinated flower?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: feathery stigmas hanging outside. Wind-pollinated flowers have feathery stigmas outside the flower to catch drifting pollen; the other options describe insect-pollinated flowers.

questions.question: Fertilisation happens when:
options: a bee lands on a flower
the male sex cell joins the egg cell in the ovule
questions.answer: the male sex cell joins the egg cell in the ovule
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the male sex cell joins the egg cell in the ovule. That joining is fertilisation.

questions.question: After fertilisation, the ovule becomes the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: seed. Each fertilised ovule grows into a seed.

questions.question: After fertilisation, the ovary becomes the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: fruit. The ovary ripens into the fruit that surrounds and protects the seeds.

questions.question: Which three conditions does a seed need to germinate?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: water, warmth, and oxygen. Seeds do not need light or soil to germinate; they feed on their own food store.

questions.question: A farmer joins a sweet-fruiting shoot onto a strong-rooted plant. This is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: grafting. Joining a scion onto a rootstock is grafting, a form of asexual reproduction.

questions.question: Maize and sorghum along the Jubba River are mainly pollinated by:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: wind. Maize and sorghum are wind-pollinated cereal crops.

questions.question: Which is an example of asexual reproduction?
options: a mango grown from a stone
a banana plant sending up a new shoot
questions.answer: a banana plant sending up a new shoot
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a banana plant sending up a new shoot. That is one parent making a clone — asexual reproduction.

selfAssessment: I can explain the difference between asexual and sexual reproduction in plants, and give examples of each.
I can describe several methods of asexual reproduction in plants, including runners, bulbs, tubers, and how farmers use cuttings and grafting.
I can name and label the main parts of an insect-pollinated flower and state the job of each part.
I can explain the difference between the male parts (stamen) and the female parts (carpel) of a flower.
I can describe pollination and distinguish between insect pollination and wind pollination.
I can explain fertilisation in a flowering plant and what happens after it.
I can describe how seeds and fruits form from a fertilised flower.
I can explain why seed dispersal matters and describe the main methods plants use to spread their seeds.
